name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
هَـٰذَا بَصَـٰٓئِرُ لِلنَّاسِ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ (٢٠)
```

```english
These are eye-openers for mankind, and guidance and mercy for a people who have certainty. (20)
```

```arabic
أَمْ حَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱجْتَرَحُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ أَن نَّجْعَلَهُمْ كَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ سَوَآءً مَّحْيَاهُمْ وَمَمَاتُهُمْ ۚ سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ (٢١)
```

```english
Do those who have perpetrated misdeeds suppose that We shall treat them like those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their life and death being equal? Evil is the judgement that they make! (21)
```

```arabic
وَخَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلِتُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٢٢)
```

```english
Allah created the heavens and the earth with consummate wisdom, so that every soul may be requited for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged. (22)
```

```arabic
أَفَرَءَيْتَ مَنِ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَـٰهَهُۥ هَوَىٰهُ وَأَضَلَّهُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ وَخَتَمَ عَلَىٰ سَمْعِهِۦ وَقَلْبِهِۦ وَجَعَلَ عَلَىٰ بَصَرِهِۦ غِشَـٰوَةً فَمَن يَهْدِيهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٣)
```

```english
Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god and whom Allah has led astray knowingly, set a seal upon his hearing and heart, and put a blindfold on his sight? So who will guide him after Allah [has consigned him to error]? Will you not then take admonition? (23)
```

```arabic
وَقَالُوا۟ مَا هِىَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا ٱلدُّنْيَا نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيَا وَمَا يُهْلِكُنَآ إِلَّا ٱلدَّهْرُ ۚ وَمَا لَهُم بِذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ ۖ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ (٢٤)
```

```english
They say, ‘There is nothing but the life of this world: we live and we die, and nothing but time destroys us.’ But they do not have any knowledge of that, and they only make conjectures. (24)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ مَّا كَانَ حُجَّتَهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ ٱئْتُوا۟ بِـَٔابَآئِنَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٢٥)
```

```english
When Our manifest signs are recited to them, their only argument is to say, ‘Bring our fathers back [to life], if you are truthful.’ (25)
```

```arabic
قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يُحْيِيكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يَجْمَعُكُمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٢٦)
```

```english
Say, ‘It is Allah who gives you life, then He makes you die. Then He will gather you on the Day of Resurrection, in which there is no doubt. But most people do not know.’ (26)
```

```arabic
وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَخْسَرُ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ (٢٧)
```

```english
To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and when the Hour sets in, the falsifiers will be losers on that day. (27)
```

```arabic
وَتَرَىٰ كُلَّ أُمَّةٍ جَاثِيَةً ۚ كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعَىٰٓ إِلَىٰ كِتَـٰبِهَا ٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٢٨)
```

```english
And you will see every nation fallen on its knees. Every nation will be summoned to its book: ‘Today you will be requited for what you used to do. (28)
```

```arabic
هَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبُنَا يَنطِقُ عَلَيْكُم بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنسِخُ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٢٩)
```

```english
This is Our book, which speaks truly against you. Indeed We used to record what you used to do.’ (29)
```

```arabic
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٣٠)
```

```english
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their Lord will admit them into His mercy. That is the manifest success! (30)
```

```arabic
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَفَلَمْ تَكُنْ ءَايَـٰتِى تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ وَكُنتُمْ قَوْمًا مُّجْرِمِينَ (٣١)
```

```english
But as for the faithless, [they will be asked,] ‘Were not My signs recited to you? But you were disdainful, and you were a guilty lot. (31)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا قِيلَ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَٱلسَّاعَةُ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهَا قُلْتُم مَّا نَدْرِى مَا ٱلسَّاعَةُ إِن نَّظُنُّ إِلَّا ظَنًّا وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُسْتَيْقِنِينَ (٣٢)
```

```english
When it was said, ‘‘Allah’s promise is indeed true, and there is no doubt about the Hour,’’ you said, ‘‘We do not know what the Hour is. We know nothing beyond conjectures, and we do not possess any certainty.’’ ’ (32)
```

```arabic
وَبَدَا لَهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتُ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٣٣)
```

```english
The evils of what they had done will appear to them, and they will be besieged by what they used to deride. (33)
```

```arabic
وَقِيلَ ٱلْيَوْمَ نَنسَىٰكُمْ كَمَا نَسِيتُمْ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هَـٰذَا وَمَأْوَىٰكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٣٤)
```

```english
And it will be said, ‘Today We will forget you, just as you forgot the encounter of this day of yours. The Fire will be your abode, and you will not have any helpers. (34)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكُم بِأَنَّكُمُ ٱتَّخَذْتُمْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ هُزُوًا وَغَرَّتْكُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا يُخْرَجُونَ مِنْهَا وَلَا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ (٣٥)
```

```english
That is because you took the signs of Allah in derision, and the life of the world had deceived you.’ So today, they will not be brought out of it, nor will they be asked to propitiate [Allah]. (35)
```

```arabic
فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْحَمْدُ رَبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَرَبِّ ٱلْأَرْضِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٣٦)
```

```english
So all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of the heavens and the Lord of the earth, the Lord of all the worlds. (36)
```

```arabic
وَلَهُ ٱلْكِبْرِيَآءُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٣٧)
```

```english
To Him belongs all supremacy in the heavens and the earth, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (37)
```

## (Commentary)

Having alluded to placing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) upon a prescribed course (شريعة) of the affair—which is the legislation of the Islamic Law—He points out in these verses that it consists of insights (بصائر) for the people, through which they perceive what they must follow of the path of the good life in this world, followed by the felicity of the life of the Hereafter; and it is a guidance and a mercy for a people who are certain of the signs of Allah.

He also points out that what drives the perpetrators of evil deeds to disdain following the Law is their denial of the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and restoration of life for judgment*. Thus, they imagine that they and those who follow the religion are equal in life and death, and that following the Law has no effect; hence, there is no fruit for the righteous deeds (الصالحات) to which the Law guides, except for wearying the soul with restrictions for no reason. Therefore, the Exalted demonstrates the futility of their assumption by proving the Return, then follows it with a description of the Return and the reward He bestows upon the righteous on that day, and the punishment He inflicts upon the wicked—the people of denial and crime. At that point, the Surah concludes with praise and glorification.

His saying, the Exalted: **هذا بَصائِرُ لِلنَّاسِ وَهُدىً وَرَحْمَةٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ** — *This is [a set of] insights for mankind, and a guidance and mercy for a people who have certainty* (45:20). The demonstration "this" (هذا) refers to the aforementioned "affair" which is the Law (الشريعة), or to the Quran insofar as it contains the Law. Insights (البصائر) is the plural of insight (بصيرة), which is the correct perception of reality (الإدراك المصيب للواقع); what is meant here is that by which one perceives. The Law is described as "insights" because it contains rulings and statutes, each of which guides to the necessary action on the path of felicity.

The meaning is: This legislated Law, or the Quran containing it, consists of practical duties through each of which people gain insight and are guided to the true path, which is the path of Allah and the path of felicity. Thus, His saying after mentioning the legislation of the Law: "This is [a set of] insights for mankind" is like His saying after mentioning the signs of Oneness at the beginning of the Surah: "This is a guidance, and those who disbelieve..." etc.

His saying: **وَهُدىً وَرَحْمَةٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ** — *and a guidance and mercy for a people who have certainty* — meaning a clear direction and an outpouring of goodness for them. What is meant by "a people who have certainty" (يوقنون) are those who are certain of the signs of Allah that point to the fundamental principles of knowledge, for it is customary in the Quran for certainty (الإيقان) to be linked to the foundational beliefs.

The specification of guidance (الهدى) and mercy (الرحمة) to a people who have certainty, despite explicitly stating it is "insights for mankind," is not without support for the view that guidance here means reaching the objective (الوصول إلى المطلوب) rather than mere perception, and that mercy refers to the specific mercy (الرحمة الخاصة) for those who are God-conscious and believe in His Messenger after believing in Allah. The Exalted said: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَآمِنُوا بِرَسُولِهِ يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ نُوراً تَمْشُونَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ** — *O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and have faith in His Apostle. He will grant you a double share of His mercy and give you a light to walk by, and He will forgive you* (57:28). And He said: **ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ الَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْغَيْبِ** — *This is the Book, there is no doubt in it, a guidance to the God-conscious, who believe in the Unseen...* — until He said — **وَبِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ** — *and are certain of the Hereafter* (2:2-4). Mercy has many degrees that vary in breadth and narrowness; furthermore, the specific mercy for the people of faith also has different ranks according to the ranks of faith, for every rank of faith has a corresponding mercy.

As for mercy in the sense of absolute goodness (مطلق الخير) flowing from the Exalted, the Quran—insofar as it contains the Law—is a mercy for all mankind, just as the Messenger sent with it is a mercy for them all. The Exalted said: **وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِلْعالَمِينَ** — *We did not send you but as a mercy to all the worlds* (21:107). We have provided some discussion on this meaning in previous sections.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ اجْتَرَحُوا السَّيِّئاتِ أَنْ نَجْعَلَهُمْ كَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَواءً مَحْياهُمْ وَمَماتُهُمْ** — *Do those who commit misdeeds suppose that We shall treat them like those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their life and death being equal?* (45:21). It is said in *al-Majma'*: Acquisition (الاجتراح) means earning (الاكتساب); it is said: he wounded (جرح) and he acquired (اجترح), and he earned (كسب) and he acquired (اكتسب). Its root is from "wounds" (الجراح) because such an action has an effect like the effect of wounds.

He said: An evil deed (السيئة) is an ugly act that grieves its doer due to the deserving of blame for it. [End quote].

Making (الجعل) here is in the sense of "rendering" (التصيير). The phrase "like those who have faith and do righteous deeds" (كَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ) is in the position of the second object of "making," and the estimation is: "rendering them to be like those who have faith," etc.

Al-Zamakhshari asserted in *al-Kashshaf* that the *kaf* in "like those" (كَالَّذِينَ) is a noun meaning "the like of" (مثل), serving as the second object for His saying "We shall treat them" (نَجْعَلَهُمْ), and His saying "equal" (سَواءً) is a substitute (بدل) for it.

His saying "equal" (سَواءً) is in the accusative case according to the prevalent recitation, and it is an infinitive (مصدر) used in the sense of an active participle, meaning "equalized" or "equivalent." His saying "their life" (مَحْياهُمْ) is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) and the subject (فاعل) of "equal," and its pronoun refers to the collective of the perpetrators and the believers; "and their death" (وَمَماتُهُمْ) is coordinated with "their life" and shares its grammatical status.

The verse is phrased as a rhetorical denial, and "or" (أَمْ) is disjunctive (منقطعة). The meaning is: Rather, do those who earn evil deeds suppose and think that We shall render them like those who have faith and do righteous deeds, equal in their life and their death? That is, that the life of the former would be like the life of the latter, and their death like their death, such that faith and following the religion would be futile, having no effect in life or death, and its existence and non-existence would be the same.

His saying: **ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ** — *Evil is the judgment they make* — is a rejection of their aforementioned assumption and their judgment of equality between the perpetrators of evil and those who have faith and do righteous deeds. The "evilness" of the judgment is a metaphor for its falsehood.

Thus, the two groups are not equal in life nor in death.

As for their not being equal in life, it is because those who have faith and do righteous deeds, in their conduct of the path of life, are upon insight (بصيرة) regarding their affair, and upon guidance (هدى) and mercy (رحمة) from their Lord, as He mentioned in the previous verse; whereas the evildoer is empty-handed of that. The Exalted said in another place: **فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدايَ فَلا يَضِلُّ وَلا يَشْقى وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْ ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكاً** — *Then whoever follows My guidance will not go astray, nor will he be miserable. But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life* (20:123-124). And He said in another place: **أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأحيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها** — *Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people, like one whose likeness is that of one in manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?* (6:122).

As for their not being equal in death, it is because death—as dictated by radiant proofs—is not the annihilation of a thing or the nullification of the human soul as the followers of falsehood imagine. Rather, it is a return to Allah, the Glorified, and a transition from the realm of this world to the realm of the Hereafter, which is the abode of permanence (دار البقاء) and the world of eternity (عالم الخلود), wherein the righteous believer lives in felicity and blessing, while others live in misery and punishment.

The Glorified has alluded to this in His previous speech by His saying: **كَذلِكَ يُحْيِ اللهُ الْمَوْتى** — *Thus does Allah give life to the dead* — and His saying: **ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ** — *then to your Lord you will be brought back* — and other such verses. He will address it further with His saying: **وَخَلَقَ اللهُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِ** — *Allah created the heavens and the earth with the truth* — etc.

The verse, in terms of its word structure and the resulting meaning, is a battlefield of opinions among the exegetes, and they have mentioned many interpretations for it. What the context (السياق) provides and supports is what we have presented, and there is not much benefit in addressing the other views they have mentioned; whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the extensive commentaries.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَخَلَقَ اللهُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ وَلِتُجْزى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ** — *Allah created the heavens and the earth with the truth, so that every soul may be recompensed for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged* (45:22). It appears that what is meant by "the heavens and the earth" is the totality of the witnessed world. The *ba* in "with the truth" (بِالْحقِ) denotes accompaniment (الملابسة); thus, the creation of the world "with the truth" means it is a reality (حق), not falsehood (باطل) or play, which implies that this world of generation and corruption has a fixed, permanent purpose (غاية) beyond it.

His saying: **وَلِتُجْزى** — *so that [every soul] may be recompensed* — etc., is coordinated with "with the truth." The *ba* in His saying "for what it has earned" (بِما كَسَبَتْ) is for transitivity or for correspondence; that is, to be recompensed in exchange for what it earned: if it was obedience, then reward (الثواب); and if it was disobedience, then punishment (العقاب). His saying: **وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ** — *and they will not be wronged* — is a circumstantial clause (حال) for "every soul"; meaning, so that every soul may be recompensed for what it earned with justice.

Thus, the meaning of the verse boils down to something like: Allah created the heavens and the earth with truth and with justice. The creation being with truth necessitates that beyond this world there is another world in which beings remain eternally; and the creation being with justice necessitates that every soul be recompensed with what it deserves by its earning—so the doer of good is recompensed with a good reward, and the doer of evil is recompensed with an evil reward. Since this does not occur in this realm, it must be in another realm.

By this explanation, it becomes clear that the verse contains two arguments for the Return: one alluded to by His saying: "Allah created the heavens and the earth with the truth," which proceeds by way of truth; and the second alluded to by His saying: "so that [every soul] may be recompensed," etc., which proceeds by way of justice.

The two arguments thus boil down to what is contained in His saying: **وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما باطِلاً ذلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنَ النَّارِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ** — *We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is the opinion of those who disbelieve; so woe to the disbelievers from the Fire. Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious?* (38:27-28).

The verse, with the argument it contains, nullifies their assumption that the evildoer is like the doer of good in death; for the matter of recompense with reward and punishment for obedience and disobedience on the Day of Resurrection negates the equality of the obedient and the disobedient in death. Consequently, this nullifies their assumption that the evildoer is like the doer of good in life; for the establishment of recompense on that day necessitates the obligation of obedience in this world. The doer of good is upon insight regarding the affair in his life, performing the required action and providing for his tomorrow from his today, unlike the evildoer who lives in blindness and misguidance; thus, they are not equal.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ وَأَضَلَّهُ اللهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ** — *Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god and whom Allah has led astray knowingly* (45:23) — until the end of the verse. The context suggests that His saying "Have you seen" (أَفَرَأَيْتَ) is intended to evoke wonder; meaning: Are you not amazed by one whose state is such? What is meant by His saying "taken his desire to be his god," where He placed "his god" (إِلهَهُ) before "his desire" (هَواهُ), is that he knows he has a God Whom he must worship—namely Allah, the Glorified—but he replaces Him with his desire and puts his desire in His place, thus worshipping it. He is therefore a disbeliever in Allah, the Glorified, despite his knowledge. For this reason, He followed it with His saying: "and whom Allah has led astray knowingly" (وَأَضَلَّهُ اللهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ); meaning that he is astray from the path while he knows it.

The meaning of "taking as a god" is worship (العبادة), and what is meant by it is obedience (الإطاعة); for Allah, the Glorified, counted obedience as worship, as in His saying: **أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي** — *Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, “Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me”* (36:60-61). And His saying: **اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبارَهُمْ وَرُهْبانَهُمْ أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** — *They have taken their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah* (9:31). And His saying: **وَلا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنا بَعْضاً أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** — *and that none of us shall take others for lords besides Allah* (3:64).

Rational consideration (الاعتبار) agrees with this, for worship is nothing but the manifestation of submission and the representation that the worshipper is a servant who neither wills nor acts except what his object of worship wills and is pleased with. Thus, whoever obeys a thing has taken it as a god and worshipped it. Whoever obeys his desire has taken his desire as his god; and there is no obedience except to Allah or to one whose obedience He has commanded.

So His saying: "Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god" means: Are you not amazed by one who worships his desire by obeying and following it, while he knows he has a God other than it Whom he must worship and obey, yet he makes his object of worship and obedience his desire?

His saying: **وَأَضَلَّهُ اللهُ عَلى عِلْمٍ** — *and whom Allah has led astray knowingly* — meaning he is astray by a misguidance from the Exalted, with which He misleads him as a recompense for his following of desire, while his misguidance is established upon the knowledge of this astray person. There is no harm in the coexistence of misguidance with knowledge of the path and recognition of it, as in the saying of the Exalted: **وَجَحَدُوا بِها وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْها أَنْفُسُهُمْ** — *They impugned them—though they were convinced in their hearts* (14:14). This is because knowledge does not necessitate guidance, nor does misguidance necessitate ignorance. Rather, what necessitates guidance is knowledge accompanied by the knower's commitment (التزام) to the requirements of his knowledge; then guidance follows. But if the knower does not commit to the requirements of his knowledge due to following desire, there is no cause for his guidance; rather, it is misguidance, even if knowledge is present.

As for the saying of some that what is meant by "knowledge" is the knowledge of the Exalted—meaning: "and Allah led him astray based on His knowledge of his state"—it is far-fetched from the context.

His saying: **وَخَتَمَ عَلى سَمْعِهِ وَقَلْبِهِ وَجَعَلَ عَلى بَصَرِهِ غِشاوَةً** — *and set a seal upon his hearing and his heart, and put a blindfold on his sight* — is like an explanatory coordination for His saying: "and whom Allah has led astray knowingly." The "sealing" (الختم) upon the hearing and the heart means that he neither hears the truth nor understands it. Placing the "blindfold" (الغشاوة) on the sight means that he does not see the truth from the signs of Allah. The sum of it all is: that no effect results from the hearing, the heart, and the sight—which is the commitment to the requirements of the truth he has attained when he perceives it—due to the arrogance of his soul and his following of desire. You have already learned that being astray from the path does not contradict knowing it if there is no commitment to its requirements.

His saying: **فَمَنْ يَهْدِيهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ اللهِ** — *So who will guide him after Allah?* — the pronoun refers to the one who took his desire as his god. The derivation (التفريع) is based on what has resulted from his state; meaning: if his state is such, and Allah has led him astray knowingly, etc., then who can guide him besides Allah, the Glorified? For there is no guide other than Him. The Exalted said: **قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى اللهِ هُوَ الْهُدى** — *Say, “Indeed it is the guidance of Allah which is [true] guidance”* (2:120). And He said: **وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ هادٍ** — *and whomever Allah leads astray, has no guide* (40:33).

His saying: **أَفَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ** — *Will you not then take admonition?* — meaning: Will you not reflect on his state and remember that these people have no way to guidance while following desire, so that you may take heed?

His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالُوا ما هِيَ إِلَّا حَياتُنَا الدُّنْيا نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيا وَما يُهْلِكُنا إِلَّا الدَّهْرُ** — *They say, “There is nothing but the life of this world: we live and we die, and nothing destroys us but time”* (45:24) — until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: Time (الدهر) is originally a name for the duration of the world from the beginning of its existence to its end; based on this is the saying of the Exalted: **هَلْ أَتى عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ حِينٌ مِنَ الدَّهْرِ** — *Has there been a period of time for man...* (76:1). Then it is used to express any long duration, and it is the opposite of "time" (الزمان) in that *zaman* applies to both short and long durations. [End quote].

The verse—according to what the context provides, which is the context of arguing against the polytheists who affirm the Creator but deny the Return—is a narration of the speech of the polytheists in their denial of the Return, not the speech of the materialists (الدهريين) who attribute events, in existence and non-existence, to time and deny both the Origin and the Return altogether, as they were not mentioned in the previous verses.

So their saying: "There is nothing but the life of this world" (ما هِيَ إِلَّا حَياتُنَا الدُّنْيا)—the pronoun refers to life; meaning: there is no life for us except our worldly life, no life beyond it. Thus, there is no existence for what the divine religion claims regarding the Resurrection and the life of the Hereafter. This is the supporting evidence for the view that what is meant by His saying: "we die and we live" (نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيا) is: some of us die and others of us live, whereby the survival of the human race continues through the death of the predecessors and the life of the successors. This is somewhat supported by His saying afterward: "and nothing destroys us but time" (وَما يُهْلِكُنا إِلَّا الدَّهْرُ), which suggests continuity.

The meaning is: The polytheists said: Life is nothing but our worldly life in which we live in this world; some of us—the predecessors—continue to die, and others—the successors—live, and nothing destroys us but time—which, by its passing, wears out everything new, corrupts every being, and kills every living thing. Thus, death is not a transition from one abode to another ending in Resurrection and the return to Allah.

Perhaps this is the speech of some of the ignorant among the Arab polytheists; otherwise, the doctrine prevalent among polytheists is reincarnation (التناسخ) — *the belief that souls of those who have not reached perfection, upon departing their bodies, attach to other new bodies*. If the departing soul had acquired felicity in its previous body, it attaches to a new body in which it enjoys bliss and happiness; and if it had acquired misery in the previous body, it attaches to a subsequent body in which it suffers and is punished as a recompense for its evil deed, and so on. These people do not deny the attribution of the matter of death, like life, to the mediation of angels.

For this reason—meaning the prevalence of the doctrine of reincarnation among polytheists—some exegetes mentioned that what is meant by the verse is their belief in reincarnation. The meaning would be: "There is nothing but the life of this world," so we never leave the world; "we die" from one worldly life "and we live" after death by attaching to a new body, and so on; "and nothing destroys us but time."

This is not without merit, but it is not well-suited to their speech quoted at the end: "and nothing destroys us but time," unless it is interpreted that their meaning in attributing destruction to time is that time is a means used by the angel in charge of death to cause death. Likewise, it is not well-suited to their argument quoted at the end: "Bring our fathers back, if you are truthful," which clearly shows that they see their fathers as non-existent, their essences nullified.

Other views have been mentioned regarding the meaning of the verse that are not significant, such as the saying of some: The meaning is that we were dead without life—which is before the breathing of the spirit—then we live by its breathing, similar to the saying of the Exalted: **وَكُنْتُمْ أَمْواتاً فَأَحْياكُمْ** — *you were lifeless and He gave you life* (2:28).

And the saying of some: What is meant by life is the survival of the lineage metaphorically; the meaning being: we die and we live through the survival of our lineage. Among other things that have been said.

His saying: **وَما لَهُمْ بِذلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ** — *They have no knowledge of that: they only conjecture* — meaning that their saying, which implies the denial of the Return, is a speech without knowledge; it is merely a conjecture they hold. This is because they have no proof to indicate the negation of the Return, despite the presence of proofs for its establishment.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذا تُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا بَيِّناتٍ ما كانَ حُجَّتَهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا ائْتُوا بِآبائِنا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** — *When Our manifest signs are recited to them, their only argument is to say, “Bring our fathers back, if you are truthful”* (45:25). This is a confirmation that their speech negating the Return and restricting life to the worldly life is a speech without knowledge.

What is meant by "manifest signs" (الآيات البينات) are the verses containing the arguments proving the Return; their being "manifest" refers to the clarity of their indication of its establishment without doubt. Calling their saying: "Bring our fathers back, if you are truthful" an "argument" (حجة), despite it being a random demand after the establishment of proof, is by way of sarcasm (التهكم); for it is like requesting a proof for the objective after the proof for it has already been established. It is as if it were said: Their only argument was the non-argument.

The meaning is: When Our verses containing the arguments proving the Return are recited to these deniers of the Return—while they are clear in their indication of its establishment—they do not confront them except with a random speech, which is the demand for proof of its possibility by reviving their deceased fathers.

His saying, the Exalted: **قُلِ اللهُ يُحْيِيكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يَجْمَعُكُمْ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** — *Say, “It is Allah who gives you life, then He makes you die, then He will gather you on the Day of Resurrection, in which there is no doubt. But most people do not know”* (45:26) — until His saying — **وَالْأَرْضِ** — *and the earth*. What was mentioned of their demand for a proof for an objective for which proof had already been established—even if it was a random demand that does not require a response—nevertheless, the Glorified commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to answer them by affirming its possibility, which they considered far-fetched.

The gist of it is: The One Who gives you life for the first time, then makes you die, then gathers you to the Day of Resurrection in which there is no doubt, is Allah, the Glorified. To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth; He judges therein as He wills and disposes therein however He intends. Thus, it is for Him to judge the return of people to Him and to dispose of you by gathering you to the Day of Resurrection, and the judgment between you, then the recompense. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَخْسَرُ الْمُبْطِلُونَ** — *And on the day when the Hour sets in, on that day the followers of falsehood will be the losers* (45:27). Al-Raghib said: Loss (الخسر) and losing (الخسران) is the reduction of capital. This is attributed to the person, so it is said: "So-and-so lost"; and to the action, so it is said: "his trade lost." The Exalted said: **تِلْكَ إِذاً كَرَّةٌ خاسِرَةٌ** — *That, then, would be a losing return* (79:12). This is used for external acquisitions like wealth and status in the world—which is most common—and for psychological acquisitions like health, safety, intellect, faith, and reward—which is what Allah, the Exalted, made the "manifest loss" (الخسران المبين).

He said: Every loss mentioned by Allah, the Exalted, in the Quran is according to this latter meaning, not the loss related to financial acquisitions and human trades.

He said: Nullification (الإبطال) is said regarding the corruption of a thing and its removal, whether that thing is true or false. The Exalted said: **لِيُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ وَيُبْطِلَ الْباطِلَ** — *that He might confirm the truth and nullify falsehood* (8:8). It may also be said of one who says something that has no reality, such as: **وَلَئِنْ جِئْتَهُمْ بِآيَةٍ لَيَقُولَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا مُبْطِلُونَ** — *And if you bring them a sign, the disbelievers will surely say, “You are nothing but followers of falsehood”* (30:58). And the saying of the Exalted: **خَسِرَ هُنالِكَ الْمُبْطِلُونَ** — *there the followers of falsehood were the losers* (40:78); meaning those who nullify the truth. [End quote].

It is most likely that what is meant by the "setting in of the Hour" (قيام الساعة) is the actualization of what occurs therein of resurrection, gathering, reckoning, and recompense, and its manifestation. Thereby, it is correct to make the Hour the vessel for the "day," and they are one. It is also most likely that His saying "on that day" (يَوْمَئِذٍ) is a confirmation of His saying "on the day when the Hour sets in."

The meaning is: And on the day when the Hour sets in—which is the day of the return to Allah—on that day the followers of falsehood (المبطلون), who nullified the truth and turned away from it, will be the losers.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَتَرى كُلَّ أُمَّةٍ جاثِيَةً كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعى إِلى كِتابِهَا** — *And you will see every nation kneeling. Every nation will be summoned to its book* (45:28), etc. Kneeling (الجثو) is sitting on the knees, just as *al-jadhw* is sitting on the tips of the toes.

The address is general to everyone from whom seeing is possible, even if it is directed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). What is meant by the "summons to the book" is the summons to the reckoning according to what the book dictates by its enumeration of deeds, as evidenced by His saying afterward: **الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — *Today you will be recompensed for what you used to do*.

The meaning is: You and other observers will see every nation among the nations sitting on their knees—the sitting of the humble and the fearful—every nation among them being summoned to its specific book, which is the record of deeds; and it is said to them: "Today you will be recompensed for what you used to do."

It is inferred from the literal meaning of the verse that every nation has a specific book for them, just as every human being has a specific book for him. The Exalted said: **وَكُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ كِتاباً يَلْقاهُ مَنْشُوراً** — *We have fastened every man’s omen to his neck, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book which he will find spread open* (17:13).

His saying, the Exalted: **هذا كِتابُنا يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — *This is Our book, which speaks against you with the truth. Indeed We used to record what you used to do* (45:29). It is said in *al-Sihah*: "I copied the book" (نسخت الكتاب), "I transcribed it" (انتسخته), and "I had it transcribed" (استنسخته) all have the same meaning; and the "copy" (النسخة) is the name of that from which it is transcribed. [End quote]. Al-Raghib said: Transcription (النسخ) is the removal of a thing by something that follows it, like the sun removing the shade, the shade removing the sun, and gray hair removing youth—until he said—and copying a book is transferring its mere form to another book, and that does not necessitate the removal of the first form; rather, it necessitates the establishment of its like in another medium, like taking the impression of a signet ring in many waxes. *Al-istinsakh* is the request to copy a thing and the preparation for copying. [End quote].

What follows from what has been quoted is that the object to which the verb is transitive in our saying "I copied the book" is the original from which it is transferred. This necessitates that the deeds in His saying: "Indeed We used to record (نَسْتَنْسِخُ) what you used to do" be a book and an original—or if you wish, say: in an original and a book that is copied and transferred from. If the intention were to record the external deeds performed by the human being by writing them down, it would have been said: "Indeed We used to write (نكتب) what you used to do," as there is no subtle point (نكتة) that necessitates assuming these deeds to be a book and an original that is copied. There is no evidence for "copying" (يستنسخ) being in the sense of "writing down" (يستكتب) as some have mentioned.

This necessitates that what is meant by "what you do" is their external deeds insofar as they are in the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ); thus, the copying of deeds is the copying of what relates to their deeds from the Preserved Tablet. The record of deeds would then be a record of deeds and a part of the Preserved Tablet; and the meaning of the angels' writing of the deeds would be their matching what they have of the copy of the Tablet with the deeds.

This is the meaning that has been narrated in reports from the paths of the Shi'a from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) and from the paths of the Sunnis from Ibn Abbas, and it will come to you in the following traditional discussion.

Based on this, His saying: "This is Our book, which speaks against you with the truth" is from the speech of the Exalted, not from the speech of the angels. It is from His address to the people of the gathering on the Day of Resurrection, which He narrates to us; so it is in the sense of: "And it is said to them: This is Our book," etc.

The demonstration "this" (هذا)—according to what the context provides—refers to the record of deeds, which is itself a reference to the Preserved Tablet as previously mentioned. The attribution of the book to the Exalted is in view of it being the record of deeds from the perspective that it is written by His command, and in view of it being the Preserved Tablet from the perspective of honor. His saying: "speaks against you with the truth" (يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِ) means it bears witness to what you did and points to it with a clear indication accompanied by the truth.

His saying: **إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — *Indeed We used to record what you used to do* — is a justification for the book speaking against them with the truth; meaning that this book of Ours points to your action with the truth without deviating from it, because it is the Preserved Tablet that encompasses your deeds in all their real aspects.

Were it not that the book shows them their deeds in a manner that no doubt enters it and no denial is possible from them, they would have denied it. The Exalted said: **يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَراً وَما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ تَوَدُّ لَوْ أَنَّ بَيْنَها وَبَيْنَهُ أَمَداً بَعِيداً** — *The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil it has done, it will wish there were a great distance between it and that [day]* (3:30).

The scholars have other opinions regarding the verse:

Among them is what was said: that the verse is from the speech of the angels, not from the speech of Allah, and the meaning of *istinsakh* is writing. The meaning would be: This—meaning the record of deeds—is our book, the assembly of angels who write the deeds, bearing witness against you with the truth; indeed, we used to write what you used to do.

The problem with this is that its being from the speech of the angels is far-fetched from the context, besides the fact that *istinsakh* being in the sense of absolute writing is not established linguistically.

Among them: that the verse is from the speech of Allah, and the demonstration "this" refers to the record of deeds—and it was said: to the Preserved Tablet—and *istinsakh* is in the sense of requesting to write (استكتاب) absolutely.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْمُبِينُ** — *As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their Lord will admit them into His mercy. That is the manifest success* (45:30). This is a detailing of the state of people on that day according to their differences in felicity and misery, reward and punishment. The felicitous who are rewarded are those who have faith and do righteous deeds; and the miserable who are punished are those who disbelieved from among the arrogant criminals.

What is meant by mercy (الرحمة) is the divine outpouring that brings felicity to whoever is established within it, and among it is Paradise. The manifest success (الفوز المبين) is the clear prosperity. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَفَلَمْ تَكُنْ آياتِي تُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ فَاسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ وَكُنْتُمْ قَوْماً مُجْرِمِينَ** — *But as for those who disbelieve [they will be told]: “Were not My signs recited to you? But you were arrogant and you were a guilty lot”* (45:31). What is meant by "those who disbelieve" are those characterized by disbelief out of denial and rejection, as evidenced by His saying: "Were not My signs recited to you? But you were arrogant," etc.

The *fa* in "Were not" (أَفَلَمْ تَكُنْ) is for derivation (التفريع), indicating a suppressed clause upon which it is derived, which is the answer to "as for" (لما). The estimation is: "Then it is said to them: Were not My signs recited to you?" What is meant by the "signs" are the divine arguments delivered to them through revelation and invitation. The criminal (المجرم) is the one characterized by crime (الأجرام), which is sin.

The meaning is: As for those who disbelieved, rejecting the truth despite its manifestation, it is said to them by way of rebuke and reprimand: Were not My arguments read and explained to you in the world, but you were too arrogant to accept them and were a sinful people?

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذا قِيلَ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌّ وَالسَّاعَةُ لا رَيْبَ فِيها قُلْتُمْ ما نَدْرِي مَا السَّاعَةُ** — *When it was said, “Allah’s promise is indeed true, and there is no doubt about the Hour,” you said, “We do not know what the Hour is”* (45:32), etc. What is meant by the "promise" (الوعد) is the promised thing (الموعود), which is what Allah promised by the tongues of His messengers regarding the Resurrection and recompense; thus, His saying: "and there is no doubt about the Hour" is an explanatory coordination. It is also possible that "promise" is intended in its infinitive sense.

Their saying: "We do not know what the Hour is" (ما نَدْرِي مَا السَّاعَةُ) means that it is not understood by them, while they are people of understanding and discernment; so it is a metaphor for it being an irrational matter—for if it were rational, they would have known it.

His saying: **إِنْ نَظُنُّ إِلَّا ظَنًّا وَما نَحْنُ بِمُسْتَيْقِنِينَ** — *“We only conjecture, and we are not certain”* — meaning it is not something we are sure of or certain about; rather, we hold a conjecture that we cannot rely upon. Thus, in their saying: "We do not know what the Hour is," etc., following the recitation of the manifest signs to them, is the most heinous obstinacy against the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَبَدا لَهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما عَمِلُوا وَحاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ** — *The evils of what they had done will appear to them, and they will be besieged by what they used to deride* (45:33). The addition of "evils" (سيئات) to "what they had done" (ما عَمِلُوا) is explanatory or in the sense of "from." What is meant by "what they had done" is the genus of what they did; meaning: their evil deeds appeared to them, or the evils from their deeds. Thus, the verse is in the sense of His saying: **يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَراً وَما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ** — *The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil it has done* (3:30).

The verse is among the verses indicating the embodiment of deeds (تمثل الأعمال). It was said that there is an omission in the speech, the estimation being: "And the recompense for the evils of what they had done appeared to them."

His saying: **وَحاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ** — *and they will be besieged by what they used to deride* — meaning the punishment they used to mock in the world when they were warned of it by the tongues of the prophets and messengers descended upon them.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَقِيلَ الْيَوْمَ نَنْساكُمْ كَما نَسِيتُمْ لِقاءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هذا وَمَأْواكُمُ النَّارُ وَما لَكُمْ مِنْ ناصِرِينَ** — *It will be said, “Today We will forget you just as you forgot the meeting of this day of yours. Your abode will be the Fire, and you will have no helpers”* (45:34). Forgetting (النسيان) is a metaphor for turning away and abandoning. Thus, the forgetting of them by the Exalted on the Day of Resurrection is His turning away from them and His abandoning them in its hardships and terrors; and their forgetting the meeting of that day of theirs in the world was their turning away from remembering it and their abandoning the preparation for meeting it. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **ذلِكُمْ بِأَنَّكُمُ اتَّخَذْتُمْ آياتِ اللهِ هُزُواً وَغَرَّتْكُمُ الْحياةُ الدُّنْيا** — *“That is because you took the signs of Allah in derision, and the life of the world deceived you”* (45:35), etc. The demonstration "that" (ذلِكُمْ) refers to what was mentioned of their punishment—the appearance of evil deeds and the descent of punishment. Derision (الهزء) is the mockery that is made, and the *ba* denotes causality.

The meaning is: That punishment which descends upon you is because you took the signs of Allah as a mockery that you derided, and because the life of the world deceived you, so you clung to it and became attached to it.

His saying: **فَالْيَوْمَ لا يُخْرَجُونَ مِنْها وَلا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ** — *So today they will not be brought out of it, nor will they be asked to satisfy [Allah]* — the address is shifted from them to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The speech contains the summary of what will befall them of punishment on that day, which is eternity in the Fire and the non-acceptance of excuses from them.

Seeking to satisfy (الاستعتاب) is the request for favor and making amends; the negation of being asked to satisfy is a metaphor for the non-acceptance of excuses.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَلِلَّهِ الْحَمْدُ رَبِّ السَّماواتِ وَرَبِّ الْأَرْضِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** — *So all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of the heavens and the Lord of the earth, the Lord of all the worlds* (45:36). This is a praise of Him, the Exalted, derived from what preceded in the Surah regarding His being the Creator of the heavens and the earth and what is between them, and the Disposer of the affairs of all. Among His wondrous disposal is the creation of all with truth, which entails the day of return to Him and the recompense for deeds; and this necessitates the establishment of Laws that lead to felicity and reward, followed by the gathering for the Day of Gathering, then the recompense and the establishment of all upon mercy and justice by giving everything what it deserves. Thus, He has not disposed except with a beautiful disposal, and He has not acted except with a praised act; so to Him belongs all praise.

He repeated "the Lord" (الرب), saying: **رَبِّ السَّماواتِ وَرَبِّ الْأَرْضِ** (the Lord of the heavens and the Lord of the earth), then substituted for them His saying: **رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** (the Lord of all the worlds), in order to explicitly state the encompassment of Lordship (الربوبية) over all. For if "the Lord of all the worlds" had been brought and sufficed with, it might have been imagined that He is the Lord of the collective, but for the heavens specifically there is another lord, and for the earth alone another lord, as the polytheists sometimes claimed. Likewise, if He had sufficed with "the heavens and the earth," it would not have been explicit in His Lordship over other than them; and similarly if He had sufficed with one of them.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَهُ الْكِبْرِياءُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** — *To Him belongs all majesty in the heavens and the earth, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise* (45:37). Majesty (الكبرياء), according to what is from al-Raghib, is: rising above submission. From Ibn al-Athir: greatness and sovereignty. In *al-Majma'*: the subduing authority, the subduing greatness, and grandeur and loftiness.

In any case, it is more profound in meaning than "greatness" (الكبر) and is used for non-sensory greatness; its reference is to the perfection of His existence and the infinity of His perfection.

His saying: **وَلَهُ الْكِبْرِياءُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** — *To Him belongs all majesty in the heavens and the earth* — meaning to Him belongs majesty in every place; nothing therein can exalt itself over Him, nor can anything belittle Him. The advancement of the predicate in "To Him belongs all majesty" (وَلَهُ الْكِبْرِياءُ) denotes exclusivity, as in His saying: "So all praise belongs to Allah" (فَلِلَّهِ الْحَمْدُ).

His saying: **وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** — *and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise* — meaning the Victorious who is never defeated in what He intends of creation and disposal in this world and the Hereafter, and the One who builds His creation and disposal upon wisdom and precision.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ** — *Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god* — he said: "It was revealed concerning the Quraysh; whenever they desired something, they worshipped it."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "A man from the Arabs used to worship a stone—then if he saw one better than it, he would take it and cast away the other—so Allah revealed: **أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ**."

In *al-Majma'*, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **وَما يُهْلِكُنا إِلَّا الدَّهْرُ** — *and nothing destroys us but time* — it has been narrated in a hadith from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he said: **لا تسبوا الدهر فإن الله هو الدهر** — "Do not revile time, for indeed Allah is time."

I say: Al-Tabarsi said after citing the hadith: "Its interpretation is that the people of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) used to attribute the devastating events and the descending calamities to time, saying: 'Time did such-and-such,' and they used to revile time. So he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed, the Doer of these matters is Allah, so do not revile their Doer.'" [End quote]. This view is supported by the following narration.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Asma' wa al-Sifat* narrated from Abu Hurayrah who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, said: Let not the son of Adam say, reviling time: "O disappointment of time!" for I am time; I send the night and the day, and if I wish, I withdraw them.'"

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **هذا كِتابُنا يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِ** — *This is Our book, which speaks against you with the truth* — the verse: My father narrated to me from Ibn Abi Umayr from Abd al-Rahim al-Qasir from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) who said: "I asked him about **ن وَالْقَلَمِ** (Nun. By the Pen). He said: 'Indeed, Allah created the Pen from a tree in Paradise called *al-Khuld* (Eternity)—then He said to a river in Paradise: "Be ink," and the river solidified—and it was whiter than snow and sweeter than honey. Then He said to the Pen: "Write." It said: "O Lord, what shall I write?" He said: "Write what was and what is to be until the Day of Resurrection"—so the Pen wrote on a parchment whiter than silver—and clearer than ruby. Then He folded it and placed it in a pillar of the Throne, then He sealed the mouth of the Pen—so it shall never speak again.'

'It is the Hidden Book (الكتاب المكنون) from which all copies (النسخ) are made—are you not Arabs? How do you not know the meaning of the speech? One of you says to his companion: "Copy (انسخ) that book"—is it not that he only copies from another book, from the original? And that is His saying: **إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** (Indeed We used to record what you used to do).'"

I say: His saying (عليه‌السلام): "so the Pen wrote on a parchment," etc., is a representation of the Tablet in which events are written as a parchment (الرق); and the parchment is that upon which one writes, similar to paper—as mentioned by al-Raghib. The hadith has already preceded from him (عليه‌السلام) that the Pen is an angel and the Tablet is an angel. His saying: "placed it in a pillar of the Throne" is a representation of the Throne as the throne of a king having pillars and legs. His saying: "then He sealed the mouth of the Pen," etc., is a metaphor for what was written in the parchment being a decreed judgment (قضاء محتوم) that neither changes nor is altered. His saying: "are you not Arabs," etc., is an allusion to what was previously clarified in the commentary of the verse.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "Indeed, Allah created the *Nun*, which is the inkwell, and He created the Pen—then He said: 'Write.' It said: 'What shall I write?' He said: 'Write what is to be until the Day of Resurrection of deeds performed—righteous or wicked—or provision provided—lawful or prohibited—then He attached to everything its state: its entry into the world and its stay therein for how long, and its exit from it how?'

'Then He placed over the servants guardians (حفظة) and over the Book treasurers who guard it—they copy (ينسخون) every day from the treasurers the work of that day—so when that provision is exhausted, the matter is cut off and the term is ended—the guardians come to the treasurers seeking the work of that day—the treasurers say to them: "We find nothing for your companion with us"—so the guardians return and find that they have died.'

Ibn Abbas said: 'Are you not an Arab people? Do you not hear the guardians saying: **إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** (Indeed We used to record what you used to do)? And can copying (الاستنساخ) be from anything but an original?'"

I say: The report, as you see, makes the verse from the speech of the guardian angels.

In it, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas: regarding the verse, he said: "The guardians copy from the Mother of the Book (أم الكتاب)—what the children of Adam do—for the human being only acts according to what the angel has copied from the Mother of the Book."

And from the book *Sa'd al-Su'ud* by Ibn Tawus, he said after mentioning the two angels entrusted with the servant: "In a narration: When they intend to descend morning and evening—Israfil copies for them the deed of the servant from the Preserved Tablet—so he gives them that. Then when they ascend morning and evening with the register of the servant—Israfil compares it with the copies he had transcribed for them—until it becomes apparent that it was exactly as was copied from it."

In *al-Majma'*, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **وَلَهُ الْكِبْرِياءُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** — *To Him belongs all majesty in the heavens and the earth* — and in the hadith, Allah says: **الكبرياء ردائي والعظمة إزاري ـ فمن نازعني واحدة منهما ألقيته في نار جهنم** — "Majesty is My cloak and greatness is My lower garment—so whoever disputes with Me for either of them, I shall cast him into the fire of Hell."

I say: It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* by Muslim, Abu Dawud, Ibn Majah, and others from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).
